repository_audit_workflow.overview: This workflow guides you through performing a comprehensive audit of an IBM FileNet Object Store. The audit systematically analyzes document classes, properties, folder structures, document types, and usage patterns to provide actionable insights for repository optimization and governance.

phase.name: planning
phase.title: Audit Planning and Scope Definition
phase.description: Create a comprehensive audit plan before beginning the actual audit work. This ensures systematic coverage and clear expectations.

step.action: Identify Object Store name from MCP configuration
step.details:
CRITICAL: Always read .bob/mcp.json to extract the Object Store name from the content-repository MCP server configuration.
Look for: mcpServers.content-repository.env.OBJECT_STORE
This ensures you use the actual configured Object Store name rather than hardcoded values. The Object Store name will be used to create the audit folder structure: audits/[ObjectStoreName]_[DATE]/
If the MCP configuration file doesn't exist or doesn't contain the Object Store, then ask the user to provide it.
step.tool: ask_followup_question (only if Object Store not found in config)

step.action: Confirm audit objectives and scope with user
step.details:
Ask the user to clarify what aspects they want to focus on: - Full comprehensive audit (all aspects) - Document class analysis only - Property usage analysis - Folder structure review - Specific class or area deep-dive
This determines which phases will be executed and in what depth.

Create a structured folder hierarchy for audit outputs:
audits/ └── [ObjectStoreName]_[DATE]/ ├── reports/ │ ├── Repository_Audit_Report.md │ └── Executive_Summary.md ├── data/ │ ├── class_inventory.csv │ ├── property_matrix.csv │ └── document_counts.csv ├── analysis/ │ ├── class_analysis.md │ ├── property_analysis.md │ └── folder_analysis.md └── recommendations/ ├── consolidation_plans.md ├── cleanup_actions.md └── implementation_roadmap.md
This structure: - Organizes all audit artifacts in one place - Separates reports, data, analysis, and recommendations - Uses Object Store name for easy identification - Includes date for version tracking - Allows multiple audits to coexist
Use list_root_classes to get all available root class types. Typically focus on Document and Folder classes for most audits.

Create Audit_Plan.md in the audit folder root with:
1. Audit Overview - Object Store name - Audit date and auditor - Audit type (full/focused) - Estimated duration
2. Audit Objectives - Primary goals - Success criteria - Key questions to answer
3. Audit Scope - Areas to be covered (classes, properties, folders, etc.) - Areas explicitly excluded - Depth of analysis for each area
4. Audit Phases and Tasks - Phase 1: Class Discovery * List all root classes * Discover all document classes * Get property descriptions for each class * Count documents per class - Phase 2: Property Analysis * Categorize properties by type * Analyze property characteristics * Identify optimization opportunities * Assess searchable properties - Phase 3: Folder Structure Analysis * Discover root folders * Analyze hierarchy depth * Assess organization patterns - Phase 4: Document Type Analysis * Identify document type patterns * Sample document content * Assess version management - Phase 5: Integration Assessment * Identify integration properties * Document integration patterns - Phase 6: Report Generation * Generate executive summary * Create comprehensive report * Generate data artifacts * Create recommendations
5. Expected Deliverables - List of all reports to be generated - List of all data files to be created - List of all analysis documents - List of all recommendation documents
6. Timeline and Milestones - Estimated time for each phase - Key checkpoints
This plan serves as a roadmap for the entire audit and can be referenced throughout the process to ensure nothing is missed.
step.importance: CRITICAL - This plan must be created before proceeding with the audit

step.details:
Create an audit_metadata.json file in the audit folder root: { "object_store": "[ObjectStoreName]", "audit_date": "[ISO Date]", "audit_type": "full|focused", "auditor": "Bob Repository Auditor", "scope": "[Description of audit scope]", "status": "in_progress" }
This metadata helps track audit history and context.

step.action: Review and confirm plan with user
step.details:
Present the audit plan to the user and confirm: - The scope is correct - The phases are appropriate - The timeline is acceptable - Any adjustments needed
This ensures alignment before beginning the actual audit work.

Create a todo list using update_todo_list with all major tasks from the plan. This provides progress tracking throughout the audit.
Example tasks: - [ ] Complete class discovery and analysis - [ ] Analyze property usage and patterns - [ ] Assess folder structure - [ ] Analyze document types and content - [ ] Document integration points - [ ] Generate comprehensive audit report - [ ] Create executive summary - [ ] Generate data artifacts - [ ] Create actionable recommendations

phase.name: class_discovery
phase.title: Document Class Discovery and Analysis
phase.description: Systematically discover and analyze ALL document classes in the repository. CRITICAL: This phase MUST be completed BEFORE any document sampling to ensure no document populations are missed.
phase.critical_requirement:
⚠️ MANDATORY COMPLETE CLASS DISCOVERY ⚠️
You MUST discover ALL document classes before proceeding to document analysis. Random sampling without complete class discovery will miss specialized populations.
Lessons learned from audit gaps: - Random sampling can miss entire document classes and their populations - Missed custom properties and integration points - Significant underestimation of repository size
DO NOT proceed to Phase 5 (Document Analysis) until ALL classes are discovered.

step.action: Comprehensive class discovery using list_all_classes tool
step.details:
PREFERRED METHOD: Use the list_all_classes tool from classification-content-repository MCP server
This tool provides complete class discovery in one operation: - Lists ALL classes for a specified root class (e.g., "Document") - Returns complete class hierarchy - Ensures no classes are missed
Usage: 1. Call list_all_classes with root_class="Document" 2. Document ALL returned classes in inventory 3. Repeat for other root classes if needed (Folder, CustomObject, etc.)
FALLBACK METHOD (if list_all_classes not available): Use multiple discovery strategies: - Broad keyword search with determine_class - Domain-specific keyword searches - Property-based discovery for integration classes - Validation search for undiscovered classes
IMPORTANT: Document ALL classes found, even if they appear to be: - Demo or test classes - Empty classes with zero documents - User-specific classes - Integration-specific classes
step.validation: After discovery, verify completeness by: 1. Counting total classes found 2. Searching for documents not matching any discovered class 3. Checking for property prefixes that suggest undiscovered classes

step.details: For EVERY discovered class (no exceptions): 1. Get full property descriptions using get_class_property_descriptions 2. Document: - Class symbolic name and display name - Parent class in hierarchy - Total properties (system + custom) - Custom property count and names - Property types and cardinality - Integration properties (SAP, Salesforce, Datacap, etc.) - Property naming patterns (prefixes like DCGD_, SAP*, etc.) 3. Identify integration points from property names 4. Note any specialized or unusual properties
step.importance: CRITICAL - Analyze ALL classes, not just "important" ones

step.details:
Use document_search to count documents in EACH discovered class: - Search with class name and empty search_properties array - Record exact document counts for each class - DO NOT skip classes that appear to be demo/test classes - Document classes with zero documents (for cleanup planning) - Calculate total repository size across all classes
Create a complete class inventory table: | Class Name | Document Count | Custom Properties | Integration | |------------|----------------|-------------------|-------------| | Document | X | 0 | None | | [Class1] | Y | N | [System] | | [Class2] | Z | M | [System] |
This inventory is MANDATORY before proceeding to document sampling.
step.validation: Sum all document counts and verify against expected repository size. If counts seem low, investigate for missed classes.

step.details: Analyze and document: - Class inheritance hierarchy - Naming convention patterns (prefixes, suffixes) - Duplicate or similar classes - Demo/test classes in production - User-specific classes (usr1_, usr2_, etc.) - Integration-specific classes

MANDATORY: Create a classDiagram showing: - All discovered document classes - Inheritance relationships (Document as root) - Key properties for each class (3-5 most important) - Property counts (system vs custom) - Notes for classes with special characteristics
Include this diagram in the class analysis document. Use consistent styling: green for well-structured, yellow for review needed, red for issues.
step.tool: write_to_file (in analysis document with Mermaid code block)
step.importance: CRITICAL - Visual representation is required for class analysis

phase.name: property_analysis
phase.title: Property Usage and Optimization Analysis
phase.description: Analyze property definitions, usage patterns, and identify optimization opportunities

step.details: For each class, categorize properties into: - Core system properties (Id, Creator, Dates, Version control) - Business properties (custom metadata) - Integration properties (SAP, Salesforce, external systems) - AI/GenAI properties - Deprecated or unused properties

Document for each property: - Data type (STRING, INTEGER, DATE, BOOLEAN, OBJECT) - Cardinality (single vs multi-value) - Searchability (searchable vs non-searchable) - Required vs optional - Choice list constraints (if any)
Look for: - Properties with inconsistent naming - Duplicate properties across classes - Properties that could be consolidated - Missing descriptions or documentation - Properties with unclear purpose
Use get_searchable_property_descriptions to identify: - Which properties are indexed for search - Properties that should be searchable but aren't - Over-indexing (too many searchable properties)

phase.name: folder_structure
phase.title: Folder Structure and Organization Analysis
phase.description: Analyze the folder hierarchy, naming conventions, and organization patterns

step.details: Use repository_object_search to find top-level folders: - Search for Folder class - Identify root folder structure - Document folder naming conventions

For key folder paths:
Create folder hierarchy Mermaid diagrams
MANDATORY: Create graph TD diagrams showing: - Complete folder hierarchy for each major organization - Folder naming patterns and conventions - Employee folder structures with HR categories - Color-code by organization for easy identification
Create separate diagrams for: 1. Overall repository folder structure (high-level) 2. Detailed view of each organization's folder hierarchy 3. Comparison diagram showing naming convention differences
Include these diagrams in the folder analysis document.
write_to_file (in analysis document with Mermaid code blocks) CRITICAL - Visual folder structure is essential for understanding organization
- Use get_folder_detail to examine folder properties - Use get_folder_documents to see contained documents - Document folder depth and organization patterns - Identify overly deep or flat structures
Document: - Folder naming conventions - Organization by department, project, date, etc. - Consistency across different areas - Empty folders or orphaned structures

phase.name: document_type_analysis
phase.title: Document Type and Content Analysis
phase.description: Analyze document types, content patterns, and classification accuracy using STRATIFIED SAMPLING to ensure all document populations are represented.
phase.critical_requirement:
⚠️ MANDATORY STRATIFIED SAMPLING ⚠️
You MUST use stratified sampling by document class, NOT random sampling.
Lessons learned from audit gaps: - Random sampling of 50 documents missed DCGD_Document population entirely - Specialized document classes were not represented in sample - Led to incomplete integration discovery and underestimated issues
REQUIRED APPROACH: 1. Sample documents from EVERY class with documents (proportional to class size) 2. Ensure minimum sample size per class (at least 3-5 documents per class) 3. Include ALL classes, even demo/test classes 4. Validate that sample represents full repository diversity

step.details:
BEFORE sampling any documents, create a sampling plan:
1. List ALL classes with their document counts (from Phase 2) 2. Calculate sample size per class using stratified approach: - For classes with fewer than 10 documents: Sample ALL documents - For classes with 10-50 documents: Sample 50% (minimum 5) - For classes with 50-100 documents: Sample 30% (minimum 10) - For classes with more than 100 documents: Sample 20% (minimum 15)
3. Create sampling table: | Class | Total Docs | Sample Size | Sampling % | |-------|------------|-------------|------------| | Document | 50 | 10 | 20% | | [Class1] | 8 | 8 | 100% | | [Class2] | 40 | 12 | 30% |
4. Ensure total sample size is adequate (typically 50-100 documents minimum) 5. Document the sampling methodology in audit report
This plan ensures NO document population is missed.
step.importance: CRITICAL - Must be completed before any document sampling

step.details:
For EACH class in the sampling plan: 1. Use document_search to retrieve documents from that specific class 2. Sample the planned number of documents from each class 3. For each sampled document: - Use get_document_properties to examine metadata - Use get_document_text_extract to analyze content (if text available) - Record document ID, class, properties, and content summary 4. Track which classes have been sampled 5. Verify all classes in plan have been covered
IMPORTANT: Do NOT skip any class, even if it appears to be: - A demo or test class - A class with few documents - A class with unusual naming
step.validation: After sampling, verify: - All classes in plan have been sampled - Sample sizes match the plan - No document populations were skipped

step.action: Analyze document type patterns across ALL classes
step.details:
For each class with documents: - Identify document type properties (DocType, DocumentType, etc.) - Use document_search to find documents by type within each class - Document common document types per class - Identify type distribution patterns - Note any type-to-class mismatches (e.g., Invoice in HRDocument class)
Create comprehensive type distribution analysis: - Types found in each class - Frequency of each type - Misclassification patterns - Type-specific properties

For documents in each class: - Compare document content with assigned class - Check if properties match class definition - Identify misclassified documents - Calculate misclassification rate PER CLASS - Document patterns of misclassification
Report misclassification by class: | Class | Sampled | Correctly Classified | Misclassification Rate | |-------|---------|---------------------|------------------------| | Document | 10 | 0 | 100% | | [Class1] | 8 | 2 | 75% | | [Class2] | 12 | 0 | 100% |

MANDATORY: Create graph LR diagram showing: - IBM FileNet repository at center - All integrated external systems (SAP, Salesforce, GenAI, DocuSign, etc.) - Data flow directions (bidirectional arrows) - Property counts for each integration - Color-code each system distinctly
Include this diagram in both: - Integration assessment section - Executive summary for high-level overview
step.importance: CRITICAL - Integration visualization is essential for architecture understanding

step.details: For versioned documents: - Use get_document_versions to analyze version history - Document versioning patterns - Identify version sprawl issues

phase.name: integration_assessment
phase.title: Integration Point Assessment
phase.description: Comprehensively identify and document ALL integration points with external systems. CRITICAL: Use systematic property analysis to discover all integrations.
phase.critical_requirement:
⚠️ MANDATORY COMPREHENSIVE INTEGRATION DISCOVERY ⚠️
You MUST systematically discover ALL integration points, not just obvious ones.
Lessons learned from audit gaps: - IBM Datacap integration was completely missed - Only discovered after finding DCGD_* properties in supplementary analysis - Integration discovery was incomplete and ad-hoc
REQUIRED APPROACH: 1. Analyze ALL custom properties across ALL classes for integration patterns 2. Search for property naming prefixes that indicate integrations 3. Examine document creators/modifiers for integration system accounts 4. Validate findings by checking actual document metadata

step.details:
Analyze ALL custom properties from Phase 2 to identify integration patterns:
1. Property Prefix Analysis: Look for consistent property naming patterns that indicate integrations: - Properties with system-specific prefixes (e.g., SAP*, SF*, etc.) - Properties with integration-specific terminology - Any consistent prefixes across multiple properties
2. Property Name Pattern Analysis: - Properties with "ID", "Link", "Trigger", "Timestamp" suffixes - Properties referencing external system names - Properties with integration-specific terminology
3. Create comprehensive integration inventory: | Integration System | Property Count | Property Names | Classes Using | |-------------------|----------------|----------------|---------------| | [System1] | N | [Properties...] | [Classes...] | | [System2] | M | [Properties...] | [Classes...] |
IMPORTANT: Review properties from ALL classes discovered in Phase 2.
step.tool: Review class property descriptions from Phase 2
step.importance: CRITICAL - Must analyze all properties systematically

step.action: Document creator/modifier analysis for integration accounts
step.details:
Examine document metadata to identify integration system accounts:
1. From sampled documents in Phase 5, analyze Creator and LastModifier fields 2. Look for system account patterns: - Service accounts with system names - Integration user accounts - Automated process accounts
3. Map system accounts to integration systems: - Look for system names in usernames - Identify service account patterns - Match to integration property patterns
4. Document integration entry points: | System Account | Integration System | Document Classes | Activity Period | |----------------|-------------------|------------------|-----------------| | [account] | [System] | [Classes] | [Period] |
This reveals active integration points and their usage patterns.
step.validation: Cross-reference system accounts with property patterns to confirm integrations.

step.details:
For EACH discovered integration system:
1. Integration Overview: - System name and purpose - Integration type (inbound/outbound/bidirectional) - Integration mechanism (API, file transfer, event-driven)
2. Property Usage: - List all properties used by this integration - Document property purposes and data types - Note required vs optional properties
3. Document Classes: - Which classes use this integration - How many documents per class - Usage patterns and frequency
4. Data Flow: - What data flows from external system to FileNet - What data flows from FileNet to external system - Trigger mechanisms (manual, automatic, scheduled)
5. Integration Health: - Are properties being populated correctly? - Any indexing failures related to integration? - Data quality issues from integration
Example format: - System: [Integration System Name] - Type: Inbound/Outbound/Bidirectional - Properties: [Count] properties with [prefix] pattern - Classes: [Classes using this integration] - Issues: [Any identified issues]

MANDATORY: Create comprehensive Mermaid diagram showing:
```mermaid graph LR FileNet[IBM FileNet P8] System1[External System 1] System2[External System 2] System3[External System 3]
System1 -->|Data Flow| FileNet FileNet -->|Data Flow| System2 System3 -->|Data Flow| FileNet ```
Include: - All discovered integration systems - Data flow directions - Property counts for each integration - Color coding by integration type
Place diagram in both integration analysis and executive summary.
step.tool: write_to_file (in analysis documents with Mermaid code block)
step.importance: CRITICAL - Visual integration map is essential

step.details:
Final validation to ensure no integrations were missed:
1. Review all custom properties again for any missed patterns 2. Check if any document classes have unexplained properties 3. Verify all system accounts have been mapped to integrations 4. Confirm integration count matches property prefix patterns 5. Ask: "Are there any properties that suggest an integration we haven't documented?"
If validation reveals gaps, return to step 1 and re-analyze.
step.importance: HIGH - Prevents missing integrations like Datacap

phase.name: report_generation
phase.title: Audit Report Generation and Recommendations
phase.description: Compile findings into a comprehensive audit report with actionable recommendations

step.action: Generate executive summary in reports folder
step.details: Create Executive_Summary.md in audits/[ObjectStore]_[DATE]/reports/: - Total number of classes - Total number of properties - Key findings and issues - Priority recommendations - Quick reference for stakeholders

MANDATORY: Create gantt diagram showing: - All implementation phases (typically 5 phases over 12 months) - Major tasks within each phase - Dependencies between tasks - Timeline with start/end dates - Milestones and checkpoints
Include this diagram in: - Implementation roadmap document - Executive summary
This provides visual timeline for stakeholders and project planning.
step.importance: CRITICAL - Timeline visualization is essential for planning

step.details:
MANDATORY: For each consolidation opportunity, create graph LR diagrams showing: - Current state: Multiple similar classes - Proposed state: Unified consolidated class - Migration paths with dotted arrows - Color coding: red for deprecated, green for new unified class
Create separate diagrams for: - Invoice class consolidation - Policy class consolidation - Any other identified consolidation opportunities
Include these in the consolidation plans document.
step.importance: HIGH - Visual before/after comparisons clarify consolidation strategy

step.details: Create Repository_Audit_Report.md in audits/[ObjectStore]_[DATE]/reports/: - Complete detailed findings - Current state assessment - Issues identified with examples - Impact analysis - All recommendations - Implementation roadmap

Create separate analysis files in audits/[ObjectStore]_[DATE]/analysis/: - class_analysis.md: Detailed class findings - property_analysis.md: Property usage and issues - folder_analysis.md: Folder structure assessment
This separation makes it easier to focus on specific areas.

Create actionable recommendation files in audits/[ObjectStore]_[DATE]/recommendations/: - consolidation_plans.md: Detailed consolidation strategies - cleanup_actions.md: Step-by-step cleanup tasks - implementation_roadmap.md: Phased implementation plan
Organize by priority and effort level for easy action planning.

Create CSV/JSON files in audits/[ObjectStore]_[DATE]/data/: - class_inventory.csv: Complete class list with statistics - property_matrix.csv: Property usage across classes - document_counts.csv: Document counts per class - integration_points.json: Integration property mapping
These files support data analysis and reporting tools.

Update audit_metadata.json with completion information: - Set status to "completed" - Add completion_date - Add summary statistics - List generated artifacts
This provides audit history and tracking.

Create README.md in audits/[ObjectStore]_[DATE]/ root: - Overview of audit - Quick links to all reports - Summary of key findings - Navigation guide to artifacts
This serves as the entry point for the audit results.

completion_criteria.criterion: All requested audit areas have been analyzed
Comprehensive audit report has been generated
Supporting data and artifacts have been created
User has reviewed and approved the audit findings

best_practices.practice: Document findings as you discover them
Use concrete examples to illustrate issues
Consider the user's context and constraints
Balance thoroughness with practical time constraints
